If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>π</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> equal to
question: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>π</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> equal to
solution:
Given: sin−1α+sin−1β+sin−1γ=π

Let, sin−1α=A, sin−1β=B, sin−1γ=C

⇒A+B+C=π

Also, α=sinA, β=sinB, γ=sinC   ...i

It is given that, α+β2−γ2=3αβ.

⇒α2+β2−γ2=αβ

⇒sin2A+sin2B-sin2C=sinAsinB

⇒sin2A+sinB+CsinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sin2A+sinπ-AsinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sin2A+sinAsinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sinAsinA+sinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sinAsinA+sinB-C-sinAsinB=0

⇒sinAsinA+sinB-C-sinB=0

⇒sinAsinB+C+sinB-C-sinB=0

⇒sinA2sinBcosC-sinB=0

⇒sinAsinB2cosC-1=0

⇒sinA=0 or sinB=0 or cosC=12

It is given that, α,β,γ≠0

⇒cosC=12

⇒sinC=32

⇒γ=32

q: Let \(\mathrm{S}=\left\{x: \cos ^{-1} x=\pi+\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1}(2 x+1)\right\}\). Then \(\sum_{x \in \mathrm{~S}}(2 x-1)^2\) is equal to ______.
question: Let \(\mathrm{S}=\left\{x: \cos ^{-1} x=\pi+\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1}(2 x+1)\right\}\). Then \(\sum_{x \in \mathrm{~S}}(2 x-1)^2\) is equal to ______.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \cos ^{-1} x=\pi+\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1}(2 x+1) \\ & 2 \cos ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1}(2 x+1)=\frac{3 \pi}{2} \\ & 2 \alpha-\beta=\frac{3 \pi}{2} \text { where } \cos ^{-1} x=\alpha, \sin ^{-1}(2 x+1)=\beta \\ & 2 \alpha=\frac{3 \pi}{2}+\beta \\ & \cos 2 \alpha=\sin \beta \\ & 2 \cos ^2 \alpha-1=\sin \beta \\ & 2 x^2-1=2 x+1 \\ & x^2-x-1=0\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}=\left[\begin{array}{c}\mathrm{n}=\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \text { rejected } \\ \mathrm{n}=\frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2}\end{array}\right. \\ & \therefore 4 \mathrm{x}^2-4 \mathrm{x}=4 \\ & (2 \mathrm{x}-1)^2=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $0 < \alpha < 1$, $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3\alpha}$ and $\tan^{-1}(1-\alpha) + \tan^{-1}(1-\beta) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$. Then $6(\alpha + \beta)$ is equal to:
question: Let $0 < \alpha < 1$, $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3\alpha}$ and $\tan^{-1}(1-\alpha) + \tan^{-1}(1-\beta) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$. Then $6(\alpha + \beta)$ is equal to:
solution:
Given $\tan^{-1}(1-\alpha) + \tan^{-1}(1-\beta) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

Applying the formula $\tan^{-1}x + \tan^{-1}y = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+y}{1-xy}\right)$, we get:

$\dfrac{(1-\alpha) + (1-\beta)}{1 - (1-\alpha)(1-\beta)} = \tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1$

$2 - \alpha - \beta = 1 - (1 - \alpha - \beta + \alpha\beta)$

$2 - \alpha - \beta = \alpha + \beta - \alpha\beta$

$2(\alpha + \beta) = 2 + \alpha\beta$

Given $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3\alpha}$, we have $\alpha\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$. Substituting this into the equation:

$2(\alpha + \beta) = 2 + \dfrac{1}{3}$

$2(\alpha + \beta) = \dfrac{7}{3}$

$\alpha + \beta = \dfrac{7}{6}$

Therefore, $6(\alpha + \beta) = 6 \times \dfrac{7}{6} = 7$

Answer: $7$

subject: Mathematics
q: $\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x$, then $\sin x=$
question: $\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x$, then $\sin x=$
solution:
$\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})-\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x$
$\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}\right)-\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}-\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}{1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}} \cdot \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}=x$
$\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{1-\cos \alpha}{2 \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}=x \Rightarrow \tan x=\frac{1-\cos \alpha}{2 \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}$
OR $\cot x=\frac{2 \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}{1-\cos \alpha}$ or $\operatorname{cosec} x=\frac{1+\cos \alpha}{1-\cos \alpha}$
$\sin x=\frac{1-\cos \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha}=\frac{1-\left(1-2 \sin ^2 \alpha / 2\right)}{1+2 \cos ^2 \alpha / 2-1}$ or $\sin x=\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Given that the inverse trigonometric function assumes principal values only. Let $x$, $y$ be any two real numbers in $[-1,1]$ such that $\cos ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=\alpha, \frac{-\pi}{2} \leq \alpha \leq \pi$.
Then, the minimum value of $x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha$ is
question:
Given that the inverse trigonometric function assumes principal values only. Let $x$, $y$ be any two real numbers in $[-1,1]$ such that $\cos ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=\alpha, \frac{-\pi}{2} \leq \alpha \leq \pi$.
Then, the minimum value of $x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \cos ^{-1} x-\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\cos ^{-1} y\right)=\alpha \\ & \cos ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{2}+\alpha \\ & \alpha \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right], \frac{\pi}{2}+\alpha \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right] \\ & \cos ^{-1}\left(x y-\sqrt{1-x^2} \sqrt{1-y^2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}+\alpha \\ & x y-\sqrt{1-x^2} \sqrt{1-y^2}=-\sin \alpha \\ & (x y+\sin \alpha)=\left(1-x^2\right)\left(1-y^2\right) \\ & x^2 y^2+2 x y \sin a+\sin ^2 a=1-x^2-y^2+x^2 y^2 \\ & x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha=1-\sin ^2 \alpha \\ & x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha=\cos ^2 \alpha \end{aligned}$
Min. value of $\cos ^2 \alpha=0$ At $\alpha=\frac{\pi}{2}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The trigonometric equation $\sin ^{-1} x=2 \sin ^{-1}$ a has a solution for
question: The trigonometric equation $\sin ^{-1} x=2 \sin ^{-1}$ a has a solution for
solution:
$\sin ^{-1} x=2 \sin ^{-1} a$
$-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x} \leq \frac{\pi}{2} ; \therefore-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq 2 \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{a} \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \quad-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{a} \leq \frac{\pi}{4}$ or $\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \leq \mathrm{a} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore|\mathrm{a}| \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\right.$ As $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}>\frac{1}{2}$ ). Out of given four option no one is absolutely correct but (c) could be taken into consideration. $\rightarrow|\mathrm{a}| \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is correct, if $\mathrm{a} < \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is taken as correct then it domain satisfy for $\mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ but equation is satisfied. $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}>\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}>\frac{1}{2}$

q: The set of all values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, is the interval
question: The set of all values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, is the interval
solution:
Let T=tan-1x3+cot-1x3

=tan-1x+cot-1x3-3tan-1x·cot-1xtan-1x+cot-1x

=π38-3π2tan-1xπ2-tan-1x ∵tan-1x+cot-1x=π2

=3π2tan-1x2-3π24tan-1x+π38

T=3π2tan-1x-π42+π332

We know than tan-1x∈-π2,π2

Minimum value of T is at tan-1x=π4 and maximum value at tan-1x=-π2

⇒π332≤T<78π3

=π332≤kπ3<78π3

⇒132≤k<78

question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to :
solution:
We have, cos-123x+cos-134x=π2, x>34   ...i

∴ cos-134x=sin-116x2-94x 

Put in equation i we get, 

⇒cos-123x+sin-116x2-94x=π2  ...ii 

We know sin-1x+cos-1x=π2

From equation ii we get, 

⇒23x=16x2-94x

⇒832=16x2-9  ∵ x≠0

⇒16x2=649+9=1459

⇒x2=145144

⇒x=14512

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}=\alpha$, then $4 x^2-4 x y \cos \alpha+y^2$ is equal to
question: If $\cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}=\alpha$, then $4 x^2-4 x y \cos \alpha+y^2$ is equal to
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}=\alpha \\
& \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{x y}{2}+\sqrt{\left(-x^2\right)\left(1-\frac{y^2}{4}\right)}\right)=\alpha \\
& \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{x y+\sqrt{4-y^2-4 x^2+x^2 y^2}}{2}\right)=\alpha \\
& \Rightarrow 4-y^2-4 x^2+x^2 y^2=4 \cos ^2 \alpha+x^2 y^2-4 x y \cos \alpha \\
& \Rightarrow 4 x^2+y^2-4 x y \cos \alpha=4 \sin ^2 \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the domain of the function $f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}-2 x-2}\right)$, is $(-\infty, \alpha] \cup[\beta, \gamma] \cup[\delta, \infty)$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta$ is equal to
question: If the domain of the function $f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}-2 x-2}\right)$, is $(-\infty, \alpha] \cup[\beta, \gamma] \cup[\delta, \infty)$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta$ is equal to
solution:
For $\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{x^2-2x-2}\right)$ to be defined, we need $\left|\frac{1}{x^2-2x-2}\right| \leq 1$, i.e., $|x^2-2x-2| \geq 1$.
Case 1:
$x^2-2x-2 \geq 1 \Rightarrow x^2-2x-3 \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow (x-3)(x+1) \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow x \leq -1$ or $x \geq 3$.
Case 2:
$x^2-2x-2 \leq -1 \Rightarrow x^2-2x-1 \leq 0$
$\Rightarrow (x-1)^2 \leq 2$
$\Rightarrow 1-\sqrt{2} \leq x \leq 1+\sqrt{2}$.
Domain $= (-\infty, -1] \cup [1-\sqrt{2}, 1+\sqrt{2}] \cup [3, \infty)$.
So $\alpha = -1,\ \beta = 1-\sqrt{2},\ \gamma = 1+\sqrt{2},\ \delta = 3$.
$\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = -1 + 1 - \sqrt{2} + 1 + \sqrt{2} + 3 = 4$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The value of <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cot</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><munderover><mo stretchy="false">∑</mo><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>19</mn></mrow></munderover><mrow><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cot</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mrow><munderover><mo stretchy="false">∑</mo><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow></munderover><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>p</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> is:
question: The value of <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cot</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><munderover><mo stretchy="false">∑</mo><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>19</mn></mrow></munderover><mrow><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cot</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mrow><munderover><mo stretchy="false">∑</mo><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow></munderover><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>p</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> is:
solution:
We have, cot⁡∑n=119cot-1⁡1+∑p=1n2p

=cot∑n=119tan-111+nn+1

=cot∑n=119tan-1n+1-n1+nn+1

=cot⁡∑n=119(tan-1n+1-tan-1n)

=cot⁡tan-120-tan-11

=1tan(tan-120-tan-11)

=120-11+(20).1=2119

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> be the set of all solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></mrow></munder><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> be the set of all solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></mrow></munder><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given equation is:

cos-12x-2cos-11-x2=π,x∈-12,12

It is possible if

cos-12x=π    ....(1)

and  2cos-11-x2=0    ....(2)

From equation (1), x=-12 which is not satisfying equation (2).

So, no such x exists.

subject: Mathematics
q: The integral $\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2 \cot ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right) d x$ is equal to
question: The integral $\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2 \cot ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right) d x$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & I=\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2 \cot ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right) d x\right) \\ & \int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1+x}}\right)\right) d x \\ & \left.\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \frac{1-\tan ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}}\right)}{1+\tan ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}}\right.}\right) d x\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \frac{1-\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)}{1+\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)} d x=\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \frac{-2 x}{2} d x \\ & =\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4}(-x) d x=-\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \\ & =-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{9}{16}-\frac{1}{16}\right] \\ & =-\frac{1}{4}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of real roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msqrt><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></msqrt><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msqrt><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is:
question: The number of real roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msqrt><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></msqrt><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msqrt><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is:
solution:
We have,

tan-1x2+x+sin-1x2+x+1=π4

For equation to be defined,

for tan-1xx+1, 

x2+x≥0

⇒x2+x+1≥1  ....1

And, from domain of sin-1x2+x+1, x2+x+1≤1   ....2

∴ from 1 & 2 only possibility that the equation is defined is

x2+x=0 

⇒x=0, -1

at x=0, tan-1xx+1+sin-1x2+x+1=0+π2≠π4

and at x=-1, tan-1xx+1+sin-1x2+x+1=0+π2≠π4

None of these values satisfy

∴ Number of roots=0
